places.name: Ba Be

visits.text: Ba Be Lake - 'Slam Pé' in the Tay language, meaning 'three lakes' - is actually three interconnected basins: Pé Lầm, Pé Lù, and Pé Lèng. It stretches 8 km long, covers 650 hectares, sits at 145 m altitude, with an average depth of 20 to 25 m. It's Vietnam's largest natural freshwater lake and one of twenty freshwater lakes worldwide considered worthy of preservation. In the middle of the emerald waters, two small islets emerge: An Ma Island, where locals have built a small temple venerating the spirits of feudal war, and Ba Goa Island. The national park surrounding the lake, created in 1992, shelters more than 417 plant species and 299 vertebrate species. Embark early in the morning - around 7am - for a boat ride; the low-angle light on the limestone cliffs is particularly beautiful at this hour.

Puong Cave is a natural tunnel carved by the Nang River through the limestone mountain of Lung Nham: 300 m long, 30 m high, 30 m wide. You enter it by boat on the river - the sound of tens of thousands of bats (23 recorded species) greets you from the entrance. The limestone walls are covered with strangely shaped concretions, and the rays of light piercing through at the other end of the tunnel create a striking effect. Once you've crossed the cave, you can disembark and walk to the small Tay villages on the riverbank. Don't come at noon - the bats fly out at dusk by the thousands, a spectacle not to be missed if you're staying in the park.

Pac Ngoi is the most accessible and best-preserved Tay village at Ba Be Lake, with about 80 households still living from fishing and rice cultivation. Wooden stilt houses dominate the shore, built high to withstand seasonal floods. The Tay are renowned for crafting their doc moc canoes, carved from a single tree trunk and so narrow they glide between rocks effortlessly. In the evening in the homestays, it's not uncommon to hear the Dan Tinh - a three-stringed lute - accompanying traditional Hat Then songs. If you stay overnight here, your host will prepare smoked pork and forest vegetables picked that very morning.

activities.text: Dau Dang Waterfall is the most spectacular point of the Nang River, 3 km from the lake. The water cascades down three successive levels between rocky walls, then calms into a series of pools where catfish (ca chien) can exceed 10 kg - a rare species protected in the park. The classic excursion combines crossing Puong Cave then ascending the Nang to the falls, with lunch on site in a small shelter by the water. Bring a light rain jacket even in the dry season - the waterfall's spray is quite effective. Count at least half a day for this loop.

Kayaking is the best way to explore Ba Be at your own pace, skirting the limestone cliffs and gliding under the branches of forests overhanging the shore. Outfitters in Pac Ngoi village offer kayak rentals to reach Puong Cave or simply follow the shores of the main lake. The lake is deep (35 m at maximum) - wear the life jacket even if you're a strong swimmer. Swimming is only recommended at An Ma Island, where the depth is more moderate.

places.name: Buon Ma Thuot

visits.text: Inaugurated in 2018 a few kilometers from downtown, the World of Coffee Museum is a bold architectural piece directly inspired by Ede longhouses—the wooden structure on stilts houses over 10,000 objects related to the history and cultures of coffee around the world, from Ethiopian origins to Asian roasting techniques. Buon Ma Thuot is the undisputed coffee capital of Vietnam, a country that ranks as the world's second-largest robusta coffee producer—and Dak Lak province concentrates a major share of this production. Allow at least an hour to tour the collections; the interactive exhibits trace the bean's journey from plantation to cup.

Two kilometers from the center, Ako Dhong means in the Ede language 'the village of springs'—the hamlet is indeed built at the source of a large stream that flows through Buon Ma Thuot. It's one of the few Ede villages still integrated into the urban fabric of a major Central Highlands city. The traditional stilt longhouses (nha dai) can reach 15 to 100 meters in length and shelter several generations of the same matrilineal family. Ede society is matriarchal: the main staircase of the house—the women's staircase—is always on the right. An authentic café set in a 1980s-era longhouse lets you visit the interior in a setting surrounded by folk wooden statues.

Fifty kilometers south of Buon Ma Thuot, Lak Lake is the second-largest natural freshwater lake in Vietnam. It spreads across a broad valley of rice paddies framed by volcanic hills, and its shores are inhabited by the M'nong people. At the lakeside Royal Villa—a residence built in 1929 by the French for Emperor Bao Dai, a keen hunter in these forests—you can still see period photos and original furnishings. Local operators offer rides in pirogues carved from precious wood, a centuries-old M'nong tradition for crossing the lake. Arrive late afternoon to enjoy the light on the paddies and mountains.

activities.text: Designed in collaboration with Paris's Quai Branly Museum and inaugurated in 2011, Dak Lak Museum is housed in an imposing Ede-style concrete building. It presents the culture of the province's 31 ethnic minorities through photos, costumes, musical instruments—including UNESCO-listed gongs—and ritual objects. The section on the Ede is the most developed: they number around 200,000 members and are the most significant ethnic group in Dak Lak. Don't miss the French-language information leaflet available at the entrance, one of the clearest documents on Central Highlands ethnic groups.

Operators at the lakeside offer rides in traditional M'nong pirogues—these craft carved from a single precious tree trunk are one of the symbols of water culture in the southern Central Highlands. The crossing takes you to M'nong villages on the far shore, where you can observe fishing techniques and wooden stilt houses. The dry season (November to April) is ideal: the lake is low and remarkably clear, and morning mist slowly lifts over the wooded hills around it.

places.name: Can Tho

visits.text: My Tho, 70 km from Saigon, is the delta's first stop: this is where you board a sampan to glide through the Mekong's secondary branches. Unicorn Island (Thoi Son) is the region's most visited: its coconut groves and tropical fruit gardens, coconut candy workshops, and beekeeping farms welcome tour groups all day. If you want to avoid crowds, arrange to arrive in My Tho around 7:30-8am, before the tourist buses from Saigon that arrive mid-morning. The sampan ride through the arroyos, even brief (30-45 min), gives a first sense of the relationship delta inhabitants have maintained with the river for generations.

En route to Can Tho, stop in Ben Tre, capital of the province where the Mekong subdivides into its nine branches before reaching the South China Sea. The city owes its nickname 'land of coconuts' to the plantations lining the canals for dozens of kilometers. Artisan workshops produce candies, milk, and coconut oil on-site using recipes passed down through generations—you can taste and buy directly. A rowing boat ride through the bamboo-shaded arroyos lasts about 45 minutes and completes the visit. Plan to get back on the road around 2pm to arrive in Can Tho by early evening.

Can Tho is the delta's largest city, with 1.2 million inhabitants—and Ninh Kieu Wharf, at the riverside, is its lively heart. In the evening, locals stroll, street vendors set up, and restaurant boats light up along the water. It's from this pier that you'll depart tomorrow morning for the Cai Rang floating market. Take time to linger this first evening to scout the docks, dine at one of the riverside restaurants, and watch the sunset over the Can Tho River.

Wake-up at 5am—no compromise possible. Cai Rang floating market, classified as national intangible cultural heritage since 2016, is the delta's largest with several hundred boats gathering from 4am on the Hau River, 30 minutes by boat from Ninh Kieu pier. It's a semi-wholesale market: vendors signal their specialty by hanging a few samples of the product atop a 3-to-5-meter bamboo pole planted on the bow—pineapples, melons, durians, rambutans. At 6:30-7am it's peak activity; by 9am there's not much left. Book your boat the day before from Ninh Kieu Wharf, and have breakfast on the water from a noodle soup boat—it's included in the experience.

After the market, extend the morning on the water by going up the secondary canals irrigating the fruit gardens around Can Tho. These arroyos—a term inherited from French—connect villages that still live almost exclusively by waterway. You'll pass floating rice mills, houses whose terraces open directly onto the canal, and coconut groves leaning over the water. The vegetation changes completely with the season: dense and green in summer, more golden in the dry season. Local operators organize 1.5-to-2-hour circuits departing from Ninh Kieu Wharf.

In villages accessible only by boat around Can Tho, you'll find small family workshops producing bánh tráng (dried rice cakes) and bún (rice vermicelli) using methods unchanged for generations. Rice paste is spread by hand on bamboo mats, then sun-dried on racks. Families agree to show you the process—bring a guide who speaks the local language to facilitate contact. It's one of the most authentic visits you'll make in the delta.

activities.text: In Ben Tre's arroyos, boatmen paddle beneath a canopy of giant bamboo filtering the light. The silence is total, except for the sound of oars and birdsong—a complete break from Saigon's traffic. It's also the best place to see up close the stilt houses lining the canals and understand how river life actually works in the delta. Local operators organize these circuits on demand from Ben Tre's docks.

From 6:30pm, Ninh Kieu Wharf transforms into an informal night market: street vendors set up with their carts offering southern specialties—bánh tráng trộn (puffed rice salad), hủ tiếu (noodle soup typical of the delta), and seasonal fruits like durian or mangosteen. It's a good opportunity to taste local cuisine before tomorrow's busy day. Go to bed early: wake-up for the floating market will be around 5am.

If you didn't have time yesterday, return to spend the afternoon in Ben Tre to visit a coconut processing workshop. The province is Vietnam's top coconut producer and confectioneries (candies, cookies, creams) are a regional specialty known throughout the country. In workshops that local agencies include in their circuits, you'll see the complete chain: husking, grating, milk extraction, candy making. Local operators allow you to leave with a few packages as gifts.

The Can Tho River, a branch of the Hau River crossing the city, offers spectacular sunsets from the wharf or from rooftop restaurant terraces. At 5:30-6pm, the light takes on an orange tint on boats returning from the day. It's also the right time to order a cà phê sữa đá (Vietnamese filter coffee on ice, a southern specialty) and observe river life before heading back tomorrow to Saigon.

places.name: Cao Bang

visits.text: On the road between Ba Be and Cao Bang, the Phong Nam Valley is one of the finest stops in this little-visited northeast. Tay villages line up low houses built of gray limestone, topped with black tiles - an architecture radically different from the wooden stilt houses you just left at Ba Be. Corn and tobacco crops follow one another in the valley floors. Stop to walk 30 minutes through the alleys - locals rarely receive foreigners who are just passing through, and the curiosity is usually mutual.

Khuoi Ky may be the most unique village in Cao Bang province: its stilt houses are built entirely of limestone - a technique unique in Vietnam. The raised foundations protect the dwellings from floods of the nearby river, but unlike what you see everywhere in the north, here it's stone and not wood that supports the whole structure. The village has about fifteen houses inhabited by the Tay ethnic group, some dating back several generations. If you pass by mid-morning, you'll encounter women returning from the nearby periodic market with their baskets loaded with vegetables and dried fish.

The road between Ba Be and Cao Bang crosses several passes offering plunging views over the karst valleys of the northeast. Stop at the marked viewpoints to photograph the terraced rice paddies and limestone formations piercing through the forest. In September-October, golden rice paddies in the valley floors create a striking contrast with the gray cliffs. The road is good but narrow in places - passing logging trucks requires vigilance.

Ban Gioc is the largest waterfall in Southeast Asia and the fourth in the world among those marking a national border, after Iguaçu, Victoria, and Niagara. Formed by the Quay Son River 90 km from Cao Bang, it unfolds over about 100 m wide and 30 m high, divided into three separate falls by wooded rocky spurs. The eastern part of the waterfall belongs to China, where it's called Detian - you'll see Chinese tourists on the other bank from the Vietnamese shore. In the rainy season (August-September), the flow is maximum and the mist creates permanent rainbows. In the dry season, golden rice paddies at the foot of the waterfall add a picturesque touch. Outfitters offer to approach the falls by flat-bottomed boat - don't miss it.

Three kilometers from Ban Gioc, Nguom Ngao Cave - 'Tiger Cave' in the Tay language - was discovered in 1921 by French officials during an inspection at the falls. According to a 1995 survey by the British Speleological Association, it extends over a total of 2,144 m with three entrances: Nguom Ngao, Nguom Lom, and Ban Thuon. The stalactites and stalagmites display an unusual color palette due to the presence of various minerals in the limestone - ochre, white, and slightly pinkish tones that change depending on the angle of light. Since late 2021, a new gallery (Ban Thuon) has been open to the public with illuminated walkways and a boat section. Allow at least 1h30 to tour it completely.

On the return road toward Cao Bang, the village of Phuc Sen deserves a 30 to 45-minute stop. Inhabited by the Nung An ethnic group, this village has practiced artisanal forging for over 300 years. With nearly 160 forges still in operation, it produces knives, sickles, and agricultural tools sold throughout the province - and renowned for their durability far superior to imported equivalents. You'll hear the sound of hammers before even arriving. The artisans work without particular protection in the heat of braziers, in the old-fashioned way, with impressive efficiency.

activities.text: In this region straddling Ba Be and Cao Bang, several periodic markets bring together the Tay, Nung, Dao, and Hmong ethnicities from surrounding villages. These markets - Nam Cuong, Cho Ra, or Quang Khe - are not tourist markets: people come to buy piglets, sell dried corn, negotiate indigo-dyed textiles. Costumes vary dramatically from one ethnic group to another - the Dao wear headdresses embroidered with red threads, the Hmong Flower people wear clothing covered with colorful patterns. Arrive before 8am to see the maximum activity; after 10am, people head back to their villages.

Cao Bang is a quiet provincial town, crossed by the Bang Giang River. In the evening, the surroundings of the central market come alive with street stalls offering local specialties: bánh áp chao (fried rice pancake stuffed with duck meat), Cao Bang bánh cuốn reputed to be more delicate than Hanoi's, and Nung dried lạp xưởng. It's also a good opportunity to ask the restaurant or your accommodation to organize the next day's visits to Ban Gioc - departures are early in the morning.

To truly get close to Ban Gioc, board the flat-bottomed boats that local outfitters offer at the foot of the falls. In a few minutes, you find yourself under the spray, a few meters from the Chinese border, with the curtain of water falling right in front of you. Swimming is prohibited at this point - the current is strong even when it appears calm. The best time is early morning (before 9am) when the light is still low and tourists are rare.

If you have time after Ban Gioc and Nguom Ngao, Thang Hen Lake - 'bee tail' in Tay - deserves the detour. This system of 36 lakes connected by underground caves peaks at nearly 1,700 m altitude, surrounded by mirrored karst formations. The whole is classified as a national natural site. The road to climb there is narrow and winding - count 45 min from Ban Gioc. On weekdays, you'll often be alone facing the landscape.

places.name: Da Nang

visits.text: Twenty kilometers north of Da Nang, Hai Van Pass - "ocean clouds" - rises to 496 m on a spur of the Annamite Range plunging into the South China Sea. Since 1306, this pass has marked the border between the kingdoms of Champa to the south and Dai Viet to the north; in 1470, King Lê Thánh Tông gave it its current name during an expedition against Champa. During the American War, it was called "the street without joy": the French bunkers reused by the US Army are still visible at the summit, with their bullet impacts. Since the tunnel's construction in 2005, the pass road is mainly traveled by tourists. The view over Lang Co Bay to the north and Da Nang to the south is spectacular in clear weather.

Eight kilometers south of Da Nang, five marble and limestone peaks - named after the five elements of Eastern philosophy: water, fire, wood, metal, earth - thrust up from the coastal plain. Only the Water Mountain (Thuy Son, 160 m) is open to visitors: 156 steps lead to the summit, where a labyrinth of caves, pagodas, and Buddhist and Hindu temples has been installed over the centuries. Huyen Khong Cave is the most photographed - its collapsed ceiling lets in light rays that fall on a stone Buddha. During the Vietnam War, the cave served as a hospital for Viet Cong fighters, within earshot of the Marble Mountain US base. Arrive at 7am to have the pathways to yourself.

My Khe Beach stretches for 30 km along Da Nang's shore, with fine white sand and a sea calm enough from May to August for safe swimming. The Americans stationed in Da Nang during the war made it their rest beach - they called it "China Beach." Today, rows of beachfront hotels line the entire length, but the waterfront remains accessible and lively. Enjoy it late in the day after the Marble Mountains: Vietnamese gather at dusk to swim and picnic by the water.

activities.text: At the northern foot of Hai Van Pass, the village of Lang Co ("heron village") stretches along a peninsula between lagoon and open sea. Waterfront eateries serve prawns grilled on palm-frond coals and whole fish steamed with aromatic herbs - the catch arrives that very morning. Make a stop here before continuing to Da Nang: it's one of those places tourists drive through without stopping, which locals consider one of the most beautiful on the central coast.

In the evening, Da Nang's Dragon Bridge - 666 m of steel in dragon form - spews fire and water on Fridays and Saturdays at 9pm, drawing thousands of spectators from both banks of the Han River. Even without the pyrotechnic show, a stroll along Da Nang's waterfront is worthwhile: the modern, clean city contrasts sharply with the village atmosphere just left behind in Phong Nha.

places.name: Dalat

visits.text: Leaving Buon Ma Thuot toward Dalat, make a stop at Dray Nur waterfall, one of the most famous in the Central Highlands. Dray Nur means 'female waterfall' in the Ede language—as opposed to nearby Dray Sap, called the 'male waterfall.' It drops about 30 meters over a 100-meter width, above the Serepok River, a major tributary of the Mekong. The site is surrounded by dense vegetation and former swimming pools. Allow an hour on-site to descend to the base of the falls and walk along the Serepok's banks.

Dalat spreads at 1,500 meters elevation on the Langbiang plateau in the southern Central Highlands. The city takes its name from the Da Lach River—'river of the Lat,' the ethnic group that inhabited these hills before the French arrived. Xuan Huong Lake, downtown, was created by a dam in 1919; crescent-shaped, it has become the symbol of Dalat and locals' favorite sunset promenade. Walking or cycling along its shores lets you grasp the composite character of the city: early 20th-century French colonial villas, flower greenhouses, and recent hotels sit side by side amid pine vegetation.

Tran Hung Dao Street concentrates some of Dalat's finest colonial villas, built in the 1920s-1940s for French administrators and civil servants who came to escape the heat of Saigon. Alexandre Yersin had discovered this plateau during an 1891 expedition and recommended establishing a hill station. Some villas are accessible, others still serve as hotels or private residences. If you have time, walk to the Bishop's Villa, one of the best-preserved buildings from the colonial period.

The Crazy House—officially Hang Nga Guesthouse—is a surrealist structure begun in 1990 by architect Đặng Việt Nga, a PhD in architecture from Moscow University. It represents a network of tunnels, staircases, walkways, and ten themed rooms—eagle hall, tiger, ants—melded into an organic concrete form that evokes a giant tree or a Gaudí painting. The architect's stated goal was to reconnect inhabitants with nature through architecture. The entrance is open daily and you can wander freely through the winding galleries and rooftop terraces that overlook the surrounding residential neighborhood. Arrive right at opening at 9am to avoid the midday crowds.

Designed in 1932 by French architects Moncet and Reveron and inaugurated in 1938, Dalat Railway Station is one of Vietnam's finest colonial buildings. Its three pointed roofs stylize the peaks of Langbiang or the hollows of the Central Highlands mountains, depending on sources. It was the terminus of an 84-kilometer railway to Phan Rang, abandoned during the Vietnam War. Since the 1990s, a 7-kilometer section has been reopened to Trai Mat village: small trains depart several times daily and drop you directly at Linh Phuoc Pagoda. The journey takes about 30 minutes and the scenery of pines and agricultural greenhouses rolling past is worth the ride alone.

Eight kilometers from the center in Trai Mat village, Linh Phuoc Pagoda is nicknamed 've chai pagoda'—bottle pagoda. Completed in 1952, it's entirely covered in ceramic and broken-glass mosaics; the 49-meter dragon guarding the main entrance was reconstructed from 12,000 glass bottles. The seven-level main tower dominates the village from above. The whole is kitsch and monumental, and the contrast with the sobriety of classical pagodas is striking. If you take the train from Dalat station, you'll arrive directly in Trai Mat—the pagoda is a 10-minute walk from the terminus.

Built between 1931 and 1942 for Emperor Bao Dai, the King's Domain is a French colonial Art Deco villa nestled in the pines of Dalat. Bao Dai, last of the Nguyen dynasty, abdicated in 1945 and died in exile in Paris in 1997. The interior has remained virtually intact: bedrooms, reception hall, the Emperor's study with his life-size bust. The 7 hectares of gardens are planted with century-old trees. Contrast the modesty of the ensemble with the grandeur of the Empire—it's instructive about the reality of Bao Dai's regime under French control.

Less than 1 kilometer from the foot of Mount Langbiang, Lat village is the birthplace of the K'Ho ethnic group who gave their name to Dalat—Da Lach meaning 'river of the Lat.' A community of around 6,000 people lives here in stilt houses, preserving traditions of high-altitude arabica coffee cultivation and hand-embroidered textiles. It's an authentic village, less staged than some Central Highlands tourist sites, and residents are accustomed to a few visitors walking the lanes. If you want to climb Langbiang afterward (2,167 meters, 3 hours' walk from the village), local guides organize departures from Lat.

Built in 1993-1994 on Phung Hoang Mountain, 5 kilometers south of central Dalat, the Truc Lam Zen monastery is the largest temple in Lam Dong province. It follows the Truc Lam Yen Tu tradition, founded in the 13th century by Emperor Tran Nhan Tong who abandoned his throne to become a Buddhist monk. About a hundred monks and nuns live here in active retreat. Access by cable car from Robin Hill, on a 2.4-kilometer cable, offers panoramic views of pines, flower greenhouses, and Dalat hills. At the mountain's base, Tuyen Lam Lake spreads over 350 hectares—you can take a boat ride before or after visiting the monastery. Arrive early morning for the light and before tour groups.

Established in 1966 at the northern end of Xuan Huong Lake, Dalat Flower Gardens showcase hydrangeas, orchids, fuchsias, and roses in a verdant setting organized into greenhouses and open-air beds. Dalat supplies over 70% of the cut flowers distributed in southern Vietnam's major cities. The annual flower festival—held in December of even-numbered years—transforms the entire city into an extraordinary floral spectacle. Even outside the festival, the gardens merit a quick visit early morning before your departure to the airport.

activities.text: Dalat's night market is one of the liveliest in central Vietnam: dozens of stalls offer fresh strawberries, artichokes, passion fruit, and flowers—because Dalat is also the 'city of flowers' and supplies the majority of cut flowers consumed in Ho Chi Minh City. You'll also find bánh tráng nướng, these grilled rice crackers topped with egg, dried shrimp, and chili—dubbed 'Vietnamese pizza' by local youth—and freshly pressed strawberry juices. The market sits in the downtown district around the large covered market; arrive around 7pm when the atmosphere peaks.

Dalat and its surroundings produce a high-altitude arabica coffee whose reputation is rapidly growing, counter to the robusta that dominates the rest of Vietnam. The K'Ho ethnic group has been cultivating coffee plants above 1,500 meters for several decades in cooperatives that ensure direct income-sharing with farming families. Cafés in town offer their beans for tasting; ask for filter coffee—cà phê phin—to appreciate the fruity, slightly acidic aromas of this mountain arabica.

Datanla waterfall plunges 20 meters between two vertical cliffs at the heart of a forest of century-old pines. The site's originality is the alpine coaster that carries you 4 kilometers through the pine forest to the base of the falls—a descent that's both fun and highly photogenic. According to the K'Ho who inhabited these forests, Datanla was once a refuge that allowed their warriors to resist Cham invaders. The site covers 312 hectares and includes wooden bridges, hiking trails, and a local restaurant. Allow at least an hour on-site; weekends see Vietnamese arrivals in numbers—prefer weekdays.

The Valley of Love is a flower park 5 kilometers north of downtown, popular with Vietnamese honeymoon couples. Small bridges, artificial lakes, flower beds, and horses for hire for photos: the place is unapologetically kitsch and designed for selfies, but the flowers are real and the setting green. It's also a chance to observe Vietnamese newlyweds' photo rituals, a spectacle in itself. Don't expect a wild place—it's a manicured park—but the late-afternoon light makes it a pleasant spot for a relaxed stroll.

Tuyen Lam Lake is the most beautiful lake in the Dalat region—350 hectares encircled by pine forests and hills. Operators on the shore offer boat rides of about 30 to 45 minutes that let you admire Truc Lam monastery from the water and skirt the wooded banks in restful calm. It's a fine way to close out your stay before taking the road to Lien Khuong airport, 30 kilometers south of Dalat. Schedule this activity early morning—the low-angle light on the lake is particularly beautiful before 9am.

Lien Khuong airport sits 30 kilometers south of central Dalat, about 45 minutes by road. Daily flights serve Ho Chi Minh City (45 min) and Hanoi (2h15). Plan to arrive at the airport at least 90 minutes early: small Central Highlands airports can get congested on certain weekend days. If your flight is late in the day, you can leisurely descend Highway 20 which runs alongside coffee plantations and offers views of Lam Dong province hills—a beautiful visual conclusion to this red-earth circuit.

places.name: Dong Van

visits.text: The road from Ha Giang to Dong Van, called the 'Happiness Road' (Con đường Hạnh Phúc), was built between 1959 and 1965 by thousands of young volunteers. It crosses several successive passes: Bac Sum (1,200 m), Cổng Trời (Heaven's Gate), Tham Ma Pass with its 700 m of elevation gain in tight hairpins. In the Sung La Valley, white clay Hmong houses are scattered among flowering gardens - in October-November, buckwheat flowers cover the hills in white and pink. This is the landscape you see in all Ha Giang photos. Stop regularly at viewpoints - the panoramas change at each turn and the road itself is as beautiful as the destinations.

Fifteen kilometers from Dong Van, in the Sa Phin Valley, the Vuong family palace is one of northern Vietnam's most astonishing architectures. Vuong Chinh Duc, a Hmong chief who became powerful through opium trade in the region's four districts, had this palace built between 1898 and 1907 for an estimated cost of 15,000 piastres of the time. Designed by artisans from Yunnan (China), the 64-room edifice distributed over three interior courtyards blends Manchurian architecture, Hmong motifs, and French colonial touches. The walls are 50 to 60 cm thick; the columns and floors are made of imported precious wood. Classified as a national monument in 1993, the palace is open daily from 7:30am to 5pm.

The Lung Cu tower marks Vietnam's northernmost point, at the summit of Dragon Mountain (Lung Cu) at the Chinese border. The current tower, 33 m high, was renovated in 2010; its foundations date back to the 11th-century Lý dynasty, when General Lý Thường Kiệt planted the national flag there to mark Vietnamese sovereignty. The name Lung Cu means 'corn valley' in Hmong but also 'king's drum' in another reading - the eight Bronze Age Dong Son drums are carved on the tower's base. To reach the summit, you must climb 389 steps in two sections. The view over the karst valleys and Hmong and Lolo villages on both sides of the border is the best panorama of the entire loop.

Dong Van's old quarter, built during the colonial era in the early 20th century, is a small ensemble of wooden and stone houses with black tile roofs, set at the foot of the karst plateau's limestone cliffs. The style is a mix of Chinese and Vietnamese shop houses, with canopied facades that protect from the elements. Every Sunday morning, one of northern Vietnam's liveliest ethnic markets is held here, bringing together Hmong, Tay, Nung, and Lo Lo from surrounding villages - women in traditional costume arrive on foot after several hours of walking. If you're not there on a Sunday, the evening stroll through the lantern-lit alleys remains a beautiful experience.

activities.text: At the end of the day in Dong Van, seek out one of the old quarter's small cafes that overlook the limestone ridges. Several historic houses dating from 1912, with stone walls and dark wooden beams, have been transformed into rest spaces. Order local tea or corn wine - produced artisanally by Hmong families from the villages. The sunset light on the cliffs takes on an orange tint that lasts about twenty minutes and colors the entire plateau in gold. It's the loop's most photographed moment, and for good reason.

If your visit coincides with the 14th, 15th, or 16th days of the lunar month, Dong Van's night market transforms the old quarter into a popular festival. Craft, embroidery, and local specialty stalls invade the alleys, accompanied by Hmong folk performances. Musicians play the kèn lá (leaf flute) and đàn môi (jaw harp). It's one of the only authentic ethnic night markets on the karst plateau - it has nothing to do with Sapa's tourist markets.

places.name: Ha Giang

visits.text: The road from Cao Bang to Ha Giang via Meo Vac is one of northern Vietnam's great crossings - it crosses the Me Pia Pass (Mèo Pì Lèng on the Bao Lac side) in a landscape of karst ridges and embedded valleys that few travelers take in this direction. It's a preview of the Ha Giang loop you'll start tomorrow, but from the back, with fewer people. Stop at viewpoints overlooking the Bao Lac gorges - the contrast between the green forest on the slopes and the gray limestone of the summits is particularly striking after rains.

From the Heaven's Gate viewpoint (Cổng Trời), at the entrance to Quan Ba district, you get your first view of the two limestone peaks called 'Fairy Mountains' or 'Fairy Breasts' - two karst cones about 500 m high emerging from a bowl of rice paddies and colorful houses. The Hmong legend tells that a fairy, touched by the inhabitants' misery, gave them her milk to water their fields - the peaks would be the imprint of her body. The viewpoint is at 1,500 m altitude; the 360° view over the geopark opens here for the first time. Arrive in late afternoon for the light.

Ha Giang is Vietnam's northernmost provincial capital, 300 km north of Hanoi. The city itself is lively but modest - most of the interest lies in the surrounding districts. On the evening of your arrival, tour the night market to taste regional specialties: thắng cố (horse or pork offal stew, traditional Hmong dish served in mountain markets), mèn mén (corn flour pancake), and corn wine. Organize the logistics for tomorrow's loop here - car, driver, or motorbike - according to your preference.

Ma Pi Leng is the highest and most spectacular point of the Ha Giang loop, classified as a national monument in 2009. The pass peaks at about 1,500 m altitude over 20 km of winding road between Dong Van and Meo Vac - the most vertiginous portion of the Happiness Road, whose construction lasted from 1959 to 1965. From the viewpoints arranged at the summit, your gaze plunges more than 700 m toward Tu San Canyon and the Nho Que River whose emerald waters snake between vertical walls - Southeast Asia's deepest canyon. The geological formations visible on the road's flanks date from 330 to 545 million years ago, with fusulinid fossils directly visible in the limestone. Plan at least 1h30 on site to enjoy the viewpoints - and avoid foggy days.

Meo Vac is a town nestled at the bottom of a karst valley, 23 km southeast of Dong Van. Its Sunday morning market is one of northern Vietnam's most authentic, bringing together the Hmong Flower, Black Hmong, Lo Lo, and Nung from the district's remote villages. People come on foot, by motorbike, or on horseback from sometimes several hours away. They negotiate pigs, goats, medicinal herbs, and colorful textiles. Hmong elders smoke long metal pipes in a corner, young girls display their finest costumes. Arrive early - around 7am - to see the gradual arrival of locals.

From Ta Lang village or the Xin Cai port, local outfitters offer a 30 to 60-minute cruise in Tu San Canyon on the Nho Que River. You glide between vertical limestone walls 700 to 800 m high - the cliffs are so straight and close together that the sun only reaches the canyon floor at noon. The water, of an intense emerald green, owes its color to the particular mineral concentration of the rocky bottom. It's one of the rare ways to see the canyon from the inside - different and complementary to the plunging view from Ma Pi Leng.

activities.text: Before starting the Ha Giang loop, a few practical points to settle upon your arrival in town. In recent years, Vietnamese authorities have required a special permit (giấy phép vùng biên) to travel in the border districts of Dong Van and Meo Vac - local accommodations and Ha Giang agencies provide it in less than an hour, sometimes included in the service. If you take a driver or guide, they'll handle it for you. Also check road conditions in case of recent rains - in the wet season, some sections between Meo Vac and Bao Lac can be cut by landslides.

The Lo River, which crosses Ha Giang, offers a pleasant waterfront stroll after a long day on the road. The quay is the locals' living space at the end of the day - anglers, children swimming, fruit vendors. It's the ideal spot to observe the city before plunging tomorrow into the country's most spectacular landscapes. A few river fish restaurants line the banks; grilled cá lăng (river catfish) is the house specialty.

The return road from Meo Vac to Ha Giang via Yen Minh and Quan Ba is less spectacular than the outbound loop but offers different viewpoints, notably over the terraced rice paddies of Yen Minh district and the Hmong Flower villages with brightly painted houses. Take a break at Quan Ba to see the Fairy Mountains from a different angle - in the afternoon, the light is better on the western slope. If you have time, the small Yen Minh market (on Wednesdays) is less known than Meo Vac and quieter.

Buckwheat wine (rượu tam giác mạch) is the region's alcoholic specialty, distilled from buckwheat grain - the same plant whose flowers cover the hills in autumn. It's a clear, strong alcohol (often 40°), with a slightly floral aftertaste. Local producers offer tastings directly at their shop or in guesthouses. To bring back as a souvenir - 500 ml artisanal bottles pack easily and handle the journey.

places.name: Ha Long

visits.text: Ha Long Bay covers 1,553 km² and has 1,969 limestone karst islands, of which 989 bear names often inspired by their silhouette: Elephant Islet, Fighting Cock Islet, Teapot Islet. Inscribed on the UNESCO heritage list since 1994, the bay formed over several hundred million years from a Paleozoic seabed, eroded by rains and underground rivers after the sea receded. Its name - 'Ha Long' - means 'descending dragon' in Vietnamese. Sung Sot Cave ('Cave of Surprises'), discovered by the French in 1901, is one of the largest: its two vast chambers reach 30 meters high in places. To avoid crowds, choose a junk that sails toward Bai Tu Long Bay, to the north - the landscapes are just as spectacular and the boats much fewer.

Discovered by the French in 1901, Sung Sot Cave is the largest and most visited in Ha Long Bay. It consists of two vast chambers, the first reaching 30 meters in height. The lighting installed by authorities accentuates the shapes of the stalactites - one rock resembles a general in armor, another a giant tortoise; guides will point them out. It's not the most secluded visit in the bay, but the dimensions of the chambers remain impressive. Arrive among the first boats of the morning to have a few minutes of calm before the groups.

The floating villages of Ha Long Bay - including Cua Van, the most visited - bring together fishing families who have lived on platforms anchored between the islets for generations. These families raise fish in cages under the floors of their floating houses and move exclusively by boat. The visit is generally done by rowboat, early in the morning. It's a quiet moment, far from group activities - arrive before the other junks to have the bay to yourself.

Thien Canh Son Cave is one of the star attractions of Bai Tu Long Bay, less known than Sung Sot but just as beautiful. It cuts into several galleries, some of which open onto a closed lagoon accessible only by kayak. Unlike the caves of the main Ha Long Bay, you'll generally encounter only three or four other boats during your visit - making it one of the most peaceful underground experiences in the region. Wear non-slip shoes; the wet floor can be treacherous.

activities.text: Bai Tu Long Bay, north of the main Ha Long Bay, is the best alternative for those who want to avoid the tourist highway while enjoying identical landscapes. Operators offer 2-day, 1-night cruises aboard wooden junks with 10 to 24 cabins. The first day's afternoon includes a kayaking session in caves and lagoons, a cave visit, and a sunset from the upper deck. The night is spent at anchor in a sheltered bay, in a calm that the motorized boats of central Ha Long no longer offer. Book in advance in high season and specify that you want a junk in Bai Tu Long Bay.

The first evening in Ha Long Bay is often the one you remember most. Go up to the junk's upper deck an hour before sunset and settle in with a beer or fruit juice. The oblique light on the karsts shifts from gold to orange then red in the space of twenty minutes, and the silhouettes of islets stand out against an often clear sky. There's nothing particular to do - just watch. Bring a light jacket; the sea cools quickly after 6pm even in summer.

Most junks offer a sunrise tai chi session on the upper deck, led by a crew member. It's not an activity to take too seriously - half the participants join in pajamas with legs still sleepy - but getting up at 5:30am to see the karsts emerge from the morning fog in the silence of the bay is truly worth the effort.

Cat Ba Island, the largest in the archipelago at 260 km², houses a national park classified as a UNESCO biosphere reserve since 2004. More than half the island is covered in dense tropical forest, crossed by 18 kilometers of trails. The park notably shelters the Cat Ba golden-headed langur, a critically endangered monkey of which only about sixty individuals remain in the wild. If your junk stops at Cat Ba before disembarkation, a half-day hike in the park or a bike tour to the fishing village of Viet Hai is a beautiful way to close the cruise.

visits.text: Drop your bags and head out on foot into the labyrinth of the Old Quarter, where the first trading alleys date back to the 15th century. Each street still bears the name of the craft once practiced there: Hang Bac for silver, Hang Gai for silk, Hang Ma for votive paper objects. The quarter developed during the Lê dynasty era, when artisans from across the Red River Delta came to settle in guilds. Arrive early in the morning - traffic is already dense by 8am - or in the evening when the pedestrian streets come alive around the lake. Be wary of street vendors near Hoan Kiem Lake: the pressure can be intense. Better to move one or two streets away for some peace.

Hoan Kiem Lake - 'Lake of the Returned Sword' - takes its name from a 15th-century legend: Emperor Lê Lợi is said to have returned his magic sword to a divine tortoise here after driving out the Ming invaders. The lake's last giant tortoise died in 2016; its stuffed body is displayed in Ngoc Son Temple, established on Jade Island since the 1865 restoration supervised by scholar Nguyễn Văn Siêu. It's accessed via the The Huc Bridge - 45 meters of red-lacquered wood, built that same year. The temple honors General Trần Hưng Đạo, who repelled three Mongol invasions in the 13th century. Plan to visit in late afternoon: when the lights come on around 6pm, the reflection of the red bridge on the water is truly worth the detour.

About fifteen minutes' walk northeast of Hoan Kiem Lake, Long Bien Bridge spans the Red River for 1,682 meters. Built between 1898 and 1902 by the French company Daydé & Pillé for a cost of 6.2 million francs of the time, it was then one of the four longest bridges in the world and the first steel bridge to cross the Red River. American aviation bombed it repeatedly between 1967 and 1972; today only nine of the original nineteen spans remain, the others having been rebuilt in a different form. You can cross it on foot or by bike - only two-wheelers and pedestrians are now admitted. The sunset from the middle of the bridge, over the river's channels and the banana fields of Phu Xua Island, is photographed every evening by dozens of Hanoians.

The Temple of Literature is one of the few preserved architectures from the pre-colonial era in Hanoi. Founded in 1070 under King Lý Thánh Tông, it was dedicated to Confucius and housed Vietnam's first institution of higher learning, the Quoc Tu Giam, founded in 1076. For nearly 700 years, scholars from across the country came to take the mandarin examinations. The tortoise steles in the central courtyard - on which are engraved the names of doctorate recipients between 1442 and 1779 - have been classified as UNESCO Memory of the World since 2011. Come on weekdays and avoid weekends. The morning light in the main courtyard, filtered through the ginkgos, is worth the effort of an early departure after a night train.

If you only visit one museum during this trip, make it this one. The Hanoi Museum of Ethnology, opened in 1997 with support from France, documents the 54 officially recognized ethnic groups in Vietnam. The collections cover costumes, funeral practices, architecture, musical instruments, and rituals - everything you observed in the villages of Pu Luong, Mai Chau, and Sapa takes on another dimension when you see it explained and placed in context. In the outdoor garden, traditional houses have been reconstructed at full scale: a Hmong house, a Tay stilt house, a Gia Rai tomb. Allow at least 1h30 to avoid rushing through.

Dong Xuan Market is Hanoi's largest covered market. It was built by the French in 1889, rebuilt after a fire in 1994, and today houses several hundred shops spread over five floors. You'll find everything: fabrics, clothing, kitchen utensils, spices, toys, dried flowers, and a particularly lively food section on the ground floor in the morning. It's not a tourist market - it's a wholesale and retail market for Hanoians. It's a good place to buy practical souvenirs: Vietnamese spices, tea, fabrics, or ceramic dishes.

Hang Gai Street - originally 'hemp street' - is today the main address for silk and linen in the Old Quarter. You'll find fabric shops by the meter, tailors, and clothing houses that produce ao dai (traditional tunics) in two or three days. It's also where lacquer and silk painting shops are concentrated. Prices are negotiable but reasonable. If you're looking for gifts to bring back - silk scarves, embroidered tablecloths, linen clothing - it's the most convenient place in the quarter to find everything in an hour.

activities.text: In Hanoi, missing the egg coffee would be an unforgivable mistake. The recipe was born in the 1940s, when a Sofitel Metropole bartender named Nguyễn Văn Giảng replaced milk - a scarce commodity in wartime - with egg yolks beaten with sugar and condensed milk. The result: a thick, creamy foam crowning a strong robusta coffee, served in a bowl of hot water to maintain the temperature. Vendors in the Old Quarter, particularly in Nguyễn Hữu Huân alley, serve this drink in ancestral houses where you sometimes have to walk through the ground-floor shop and climb a wooden staircase to reach the room. Don't look for big signs - it's the small, discreet addresses that serve the best versions.

In the evening, the Old Quarter transforms: the pedestrian streets around Hoan Kiem Lake come alive with street vendors, phở sellers, and bánh mì stands. Walk along Hang Buom or Hang Bac after 7pm - that's when Hanoians come out to sit on their small plastic stools to eat. Don't miss the bún chả, grilled pork skewers served in a fish sauce broth with rice noodles - it's the quintessential evening dish in this part of the city.

The Lao Cai-Hanoi night train is an experience in itself, not just a means of transport. To take the train, you first need to reach Lao Cai from Sapa by bus or taxi (about 45 minutes). Departures are between 8pm and 10:30pm and arrival in Hanoi is between 4:30am and 7am depending on the chosen train. Private companies offer more comfortable carriages than state trains - 4-berth cabins with air conditioning, bedding, electrical outlets. The journey takes about 8 hours over 253 kilometers of winding meter-gauge track. This train is affectionately called the 'TGV - Train of Great Vibrations.' Book in advance in high season; lower berths go first.

Water puppet theater is an art form born in Red River Delta villages in the 11th century. The Thang Long Theater, on the shore of Hoan Kiem Lake, is the most renowned in the capital. Puppeteers control the characters from behind a curtain, standing in water up to their waists. Shows last 50 minutes and tell scenes of Vietnamese life through music and humor: fishing, plowing, the legend of the lake tortoise. It's a good way to end your stay in Hanoi - accessible, festive, and authentically Vietnamese.

Before heading to the airport, take one last stroll through the Old Quarter alleys. Hang Gai Street is the best address for silk and linen clothing. Hang Bac Street for silver jewelry. Hang Ma Street for paper objects and lanterns. If you haven't yet tasted bánh cuốn - steamed rice crepes stuffed and drizzled with fish sauce - stop at one of the morning shops around Dong Xuan Market. It's a typical Hanoian breakfast.

Noi Bai International Airport is located 45 kilometers north of central Hanoi. The road is quick outside rush hours (7am-9am and 5pm-7pm), but can take 90 minutes on busy days. Plan to arrive at the airport at least 2h30 before departure for international flights - security check lines can be long during high season.

places.name: Ho Chi Minh

visits.text: Notre-Dame Basilica of Saigon was built between 1877 and 1880 by architect Jules Bourard. Its red bricks came from Marseille - no local bricks were used. The two 57.6-meter bell towers house six bells cast in France in 1895. Just behind, the Central Post Office (1891) has a metal framework designed by engineers from Gustave Eiffel's school. The post office is still active - you can mail postcards there.

On April 30, 1975, two North Vietnamese Army T-54 tanks crashed through this presidential palace's gate - the image went around the world and marks the end of the Vietnam War. The building has remained intact since: 95 rooms, underground war room with red phones and tactical maps in place, bunker and radio equipment from the 1970s. A UH-1 helicopter used for evacuations still sits on the roof. Allow 1h30 and take a guide on site.

Dong Khoi Street - the former rue Catinat under the French - connects Notre-Dame Cathedral to the Saigon River waterfront. It's the backbone of colonial District 1, lined with villas and neoclassical buildings, several transformed into luxury hotels. The 1908 City Hall, at the end of pedestrian Nguyen Hue Avenue, is worth a look from outside. In the evening, the street comes alive with café terraces and local art galleries.

This museum assembles photos, weapons, and testimonies on the Vietnam War (1955-1975). The photography hall is the most striking: images of civilian victims, consequences of Agent Orange across generations, photos from the My Lai massacre. American planes and helicopters in the outdoor courtyard include an F-5A fighter and a UH-1 helicopter. Arrive at opening (7:30am). Allow at least two hours.

Cholon extends across Saigon's 5th and 6th districts. Thien Hau Pagoda, built in the early 19th century by Cantonese merchants, has its roof covered with terracotta figurines depicting classical Chinese opera scenes. Incense coils burn constantly on the ceiling. The neighboring Binh Tay market (1928) is the district's wholesale market: exotic fruits, spices, medicinal herbs in large quantities.

Built in 1909 by the Cantonese community, this pagoda mixes Mahayana Buddhism, Taoism, and popular religion. Painted wooden sculptures depicting gods, demons, and celestial warriors fill every inch of the altars. In the courtyard pond, tortoises bask on rocks. It's an active pagoda: respect the silence of worshippers.

Tan Dinh Church (1876) in District 3 is renowned for its garish pink facade that clashes violently with the colonial quarter's beige architecture. This bubble-gum-pink neo-Romanesque has become an unmissable Saigon photo spot. Push open the door to see the more sober interior.

The Cu Chi tunnel network, 70 km northwest of Saigon, is one of the Vietnam War's most astounding military achievements. These 250 kilometers of underground galleries began being dug in 1948 by bare-handed peasants. During the war, the tunnels sheltered hospitals, kitchens, dormitories, and meeting rooms on multiple levels down to 10 meters deep. You can crawl through a section enlarged for tourists and see reconstructed bamboo traps.

Upon returning from Cu Chi tunnels, an hour at the Vietnam History Museum in District 1 offers a useful counterpoint: objects from prehistoric, Champa, and imperial dynasties that give a longer perspective on the country's history. The 1929 colonial building, mixing French style and glazed Asian tiles, is itself worth the look.

Nguyen Hue pedestrian street comes alive each evening from 6pm onward: Saigonese families, children on roller skates, flower vendors, and the illuminated colonial 1908 City Hall in the background. It's one of the most pleasant ways to end the day after the tunnels in a light and festive atmosphere.

On the return road to Saigon, Vinh Long city merits an hour-and-a-half stop. Its covered market, on the Co Chien branch of the Mekong, is lively in the morning: tropical fruits, dried fish, and sticky rice preparations. An Binh island, accessible by small ferry from Vinh Long, is covered with orchards and palm-shaded canals - a final delta immersion before the highway back.

District 3 of Saigon, less touristy than District 1, concentrates some of the most appreciated cafés and restaurants among residents - spots in restored colonial villas whose inner courtyards are covered with green plants. It's here that Saigon creatives and artists gather in late afternoon. An hour in District 3 will show you a very different Saigon from classic tourist circuits.

Tao Dan Park is District 1's largest green space. Before 8am, it transforms into a surprisingly calm living space: tai chi and open-air dance groups, elderly men at Chinese chess under century-old ficus trees, and bird enthusiasts for singing contests - each owner hangs their cage on a hook and the birds compete for best song. It's a slice of local life that few tourists take time to see, two minutes from downtown's grand hotels.

If your flight is in the afternoon, a final hour in District 1 streets lets you see colonial buildings in morning light - fewer crowds, better photos. Ly Tu Trong Street, parallel to Dong Khoi, is lined with neoclassical villas, several housing embassies. The Garden of Mists (Cong Truong Quoc Te), a small square bordered by bougainvillea, is a beautiful shortcut between the cathedral and Reunification Palace.

activities.text: Ho Chi Minh City is among the world's best cities for street food. Bun bo Hue in plastic bowls, crispy banh mi, fresh spring rolls, hu tieu soup, and banh xeo crepes are found on every corner in District 1. For a more local atmosphere, seek out sidewalk stalls on streets adjacent to Ben Thanh. Vinh Khanh Street in District 4, 10 minutes by taxi, is where locals go for grilled seafood in the open air.

Nguyen Hue pedestrian street comes alive each evening: Saigonese families gather, children roller-skate, flower vendors occupy sidewalks, and the colonial 1908 City Hall is illuminated in the background. It's a fine way to take Saigon's pulse on your arrival evening.

Ben Thanh market, in its 1914 building, is a Saigon symbol. After 6pm, street stalls set up all around for the night market: about a hundred plastic tables under colorful umbrellas serve local specialties until midnight. Prices are negotiable. For something less touristy, streets perpendicular to Ben Thanh have pho restaurants frequented exclusively by locals.

Cholon is Saigon's best place to lunch on dim sum (ha cao, xiu mai, lo bak go) in restaurants frequented exclusively by the Sino-Vietnamese community for decades. Operators offer guided food tours that include medicinal herb shops and artisanal tofu factories. Come preferably in the morning: dim sum is served until 11am only in most spots.

Dong Khoi Street and its adjacent streets concentrate Saigon's best craft shops: lacquerware, embroidered silk, culi coffee (mountain robusta), tea and spices. Avoid shops at luxury hotel entrances - seek out workshops behind facades where artisans work on site. Quality vacuum-sealed Vietnamese coffee is light to carry and makes excellent gifts.

Ho Chi Minh City has several rooftop bars and cafés in District 1. The view of modern skyscrapers and illuminated colonial quarters in the evening is one of the best ways to end the day after the tunnels in a light atmosphere. Prefer terraces in boutique hotels to large international hotels: the atmosphere is more local.

For your penultimate night in Ho Chi Minh City, Nguyen Hue pedestrian street in the evening gathers Saigonese families. Finish at a neighborhood restaurant with a final pho or fresh spring rolls - a way to close two delta days on a simple, local note.

Several rooftop bars in District 1 offer panoramic views of illuminated Saigon's skyline - the Bitexco Financial tower and modern skyscrapers mixed with colonial villa roofs. Prefer spots in boutique hotels to major chains: the atmosphere is more local and the view often better.

Dong Khoi Street and its adjacent streets concentrate Saigon's best craft shops: lacquerware, embroidered silk, coffee, tea and spices. Ben Thanh market is a two-minute walk for final vacuum-sealed spices. Tan Son Nhat international airport is 7 km from downtown - always allow more than expected during rush hours, and 2h30 before flight time for international formalities.

Before leaving for the airport, take time for a final bowl of pho or banh mi in a quarter alley. The best spots don't have signs in English - spot where locals stop with their scooters before heading to work. It's often the meal travelers remember best, because it's the one where you know you're going home.

places.name: Hoi An

visits.text: Between Da Nang and Hoi An, the Marble Mountains (Ngu Hanh Son, the 'Five Element Mountains') rise from the coastal plain like stage sets. These five limestone peaks each bear the name of an element—Water, Wood, Fire, Metal, Earth—and are riddled with caves in which Buddhist sanctuaries have been installed since the 15th century. Climb the 156 steps of Thuy Son Mountain to Huyen Khong Cave, whose natural opening in the ceiling lets in a vertical beam of light that strikes directly on the large marble Buddha. From the summit, you see both My Khe Beach and Da Nang Airport—a striking panorama.

Hoi An's Japanese Bridge is one of the most photographed monuments in central Vietnam. Built in 1593 by Japanese merchants to connect their quarter to the Chinese traders' district, this 18-meter covered bridge bears dog statues at one end and monkeys at the other—tradition says it was started in a year of the dog and completed in a year of the monkey. In 1653, a pagoda was added on the north side. In 1719, Lord Nguyen Phuc Chu named it Lai Vien Kieu, 'bridge to welcome visitors from afar.' Arrive between 6pm and 8pm when the lanterns are lit and the daytime crowds have dispersed. From the bridge, push open the door of Tan Ky House at 101 Nguyen Thai Hoc, built over 200 years ago: its architecture blends Japanese, Chinese, and Vietnamese influences in a dark jackfruit wood frame.

Hoi An's old town, listed as UNESCO heritage in 1999, preserves more than 1,100 timber-framed buildings, some dating back to the 17th century. The historic center can only be explored on foot or by bicycle—cars don't circulate there. Wander through the alleys around Nguyen Thai Hoc and Bach Dang streets, walk along the Thu Bon River where pink and yellow lanterns float on the surface. If you pass near the tailors' street around Tran Phu, know that Hoi An is one of the cheapest places in the world to have clothes custom-made in 24 to 48 hours—order tonight to pick up before your departure.

The Phuc Kien Assembly Hall is the most imposing of Hoi An's six Chinese assembly halls. Built from 1690 by immigrants from Fujian Province in southern China, it served simultaneously as a place of worship, a retirement home for sailors in distress, and a community center. Entry through the blue and white ceramic gate opens onto a courtyard with a pond for turtles and carp, then onto the main temple dedicated to Thien Hau, goddess of the sea. The altars covered with spiral incense sticks suspended from the ceiling create a dense and fragrant atmosphere you won't easily forget. Come in the morning to avoid tour groups.

Hidden in an alley off Nguyen Thai Hoc Street, the Tran Family Chapel is one of Hoi An's best-preserved ancestral houses. Built over two centuries ago to venerate the ancestors of a mandarin family, it's still inhabited by their descendants—who maintain it and give tours themselves. The three-bay wooden structure, the ceiling engraved with bats carrying coins (symbols of happiness), the altars with ancestral tablets and yellowed photos: everything here remains alive, not museumified. Push open the door—it's one of the few places in Hoi An where you have a real conversation with locals.

Hoi An's Central Market, on Tran Phu Street facing the Thu Bon River, is active from 5am for fishermen bringing their night catches. At lunchtime, the interior space transforms into a maze of food stalls. Don't miss cao lầu: these thick rice noodles served with roast pork and fresh herbs are made, according to tradition, only with water from Hoi An's well. Bring cash—stalls don't take cards, and prices are set by looking at the customer.

40 km southwest of Hoi An, in a valley enclosed by mountains, the Cham temples of My Son are one of Southeast Asia's most significant archaeological sites—often dubbed 'Vietnam's little Angkor.' Construction of the sanctuary was initiated in the 4th century by King Bhadravarman of the Champa Kingdom (349-361) and continued until the 13th century; at its peak, the site comprised over 70 red brick tower-sanctuaries dedicated to Shiva, assembled without visible mortar using a technique archaeologists still haven't fully understood. American bombing in 1969 destroyed much of it. Arrive at opening time (6am) to see morning light graze the carved stones and avoid the heat of midday hours—a traditional Cham dance performance is given in the morning near the entrance.

3 km northeast of the old town, Tra Que village has cultivated fresh herbs since the 15th century in gardens flooded by water from the Thu Bon River: chives, Thai basil, mint, coriander, rau ram. The quality of these herbs is such that Hoi An's restaurants claim them as a distinctive ingredient. Tour operators offer bicycle circuits from the old town that include visiting a garden, a demonstration of traditional seaweed composting, and sometimes a cooking class on a family farm. The round-trip ride takes 30 minutes each way on a flat road along the river—suitable for all levels.

activities.text: During each full moon, all electric shops in the old town turn off and only silk lanterns light the alleys—if your stay coincides with this date, don't miss this event for anything. On other evenings, the old town remains beautiful after 7pm: street vendors offer toasted bánh mì, chè (sweet bean dessert with coconut milk), and rice pancakes. Along the Thu Bon River, children sell lanterns to place on the water to honor ancestors—the sight of dozens of lights drifting in the current is one of the trip's most powerful images.

The afternoon is ideal for choosing between two of Hoi An's emblematic craft activities. Tour operators offer half-day cooking classes including a visit to the central market, learning three or four local recipes, and eating the meal you cooked. If cooking doesn't appeal to you, opt for a silk and bamboo lantern-making workshop—Hoi An is one of the last cities in Vietnam where this craft is still practiced by families who've passed it down through generations. One to two-hour workshops welcome beginners with no experience. Book the day before from your accommodation.

Leave the touristy old town and cross the bridge to An Hoi district, on the other side of the Thu Bon River. The alleys are quieter there, the artisan shops more authentic, and you'll find a few wood carving and bamboo boat-making workshops that still work the old-fashioned way. This is also where local restaurateurs gather in the evening—no multilingual menus, just plastic tables and fresh fish grilled over embers.

An Bang Beach is located 5 km northeast of the old town—20 minutes by bicycle or 10 minutes by taxi. Less crowded than Cua Dai Beach (partly eroded in recent years), An Bang is the favorite beach of long-term travelers in Hoi An. Beach chairs, seafood restaurants with feet in the sand, and bars offering fresh fruit juices line the waterfront. It's the ideal spot for a beach break after the long morning excursion to My Son. The sea is generally calm here in the dry season.

End this last evening in Hoi An on the Thu Bon River. Tour operators offer outings in round boats (thuyền thúng)—these basket-shaped boats that are one of the visual symbols of the Thu Bon delta—at sunset from the old town quays. Some circuits include a net fishing lesson or placing lanterns on the water. The warm evening light on the old town's yellow facades seen from the water is a viewpoint like no other—book the day before from your accommodation.

places.name: Hue

visits.text: The Meridian Gate, known as Ngo Mon, delivers your first stunning impression of the citadel. Built in 1833 under Emperor Minh Mang, it's crowned by the Five Phoenix Pavilion whose central roof in yellow tiles—a color reserved exclusively for imperial use—served as the platform from which the emperor proclaimed his decrees. Pass through the central portal, once reserved for the sovereign alone, and take time to look up: the nine roofs of the pavilion seen from the inner courtyard resemble five phoenixes in flight. Just beyond opens the Palace of Supreme Harmony, the throne hall whose purple and yellow lacquered columns support a ceiling adorned with 80 gilded dragons. Arrive at opening time (7am) to beat the tour groups that crowd the central courtyard between 9am and 11am.

Behind the Throne Palace stretched the Purple Forbidden City, the private residence of the emperor and his family—the only people authorized to enter. The fighting in 1968 during the Tet Offensive destroyed most of it, and what you walk through today is partly a recent reconstruction financed by UNESCO and the Vietnamese government. What makes this place poignant is precisely this emptiness: the restored foundations and gardens tell their story as much through what's missing as through what remains. The Royal Theatre (Duyet Thi Duong), built in 1826, is one of the best-preserved buildings—performances of nhã nhạc court music, listed as UNESCO intangible heritage, still take place there on certain days.

Before diving into the inner complexes, take time to observe the outer rampart from the bank of the Perfume River. The walls, 6 meters high and 21 meters thick, were built from 1803 following Vauban's principles adapted by Nguyen engineers: moats on all four sides, ten two-story brick gates, bastions at the corners. The Flag Tower (Ky Dai), erected in 1807 on three superimposed brick drums, reaches 54.5 meters including the reinforced concrete flagpole installed in 1948—this is the tower featured on 50,000 dong banknotes. Allow 15 minutes to walk around the base and choose your photo angle.

Thien Mu Pagoda stands on a hill overlooking the Perfume River 3 kilometers west of the citadel. Its octagonal tower Phuoc Duyen, 21 meters high and spread over seven stories—each representing an incarnation of Buddha—has been Hue's emblem since its construction in 1844 by Emperor Thieu Tri. The pagoda itself is older: founded in 1601 by Lord Nguyen Hoang, it's one of the most venerated Buddhist sites in central Vietnam. In the courtyard, you'll find a bell from 1710 weighing 2,052 kilograms and a stele from 1715 placed on the back of a marble turtle. If possible, take a boat from the city center—arriving by river is far more beautiful than by road.

6 km southwest of the citadel, Tu Duc's tomb is the most visited of the Nguyen mausoleums. Emperor Tu Duc (1829-1883), who ruled longer than any other emperor of the dynasty with 36 years on the throne, had it built between 1864 and 1867 as a retirement residence during his lifetime: he would come to compose poetry and sail on Luu Khiem Lake from his pavilion on stilts. The site comprises nearly 50 buildings over more than 120,000 m², all named with the word Khiem—'modesty' in Vietnamese. In the Stele Pavilion, a stone engraved with 4,935 words composed by the emperor himself recounts his life and regrets. Visit early in the morning for soft light on the ponds.

Khai Dinh's tomb, perched on Chau Chu hill 10 km from Hue, stands in stark contrast to all other Nguyen mausoleums. Built between 1920 and 1931 for the dynasty's twelfth emperor, it embraces an unabashed blend of French neoclassical architecture, Hindu art, and Vietnamese tradition—Roman pilasters, reinforced concrete, and an interior entirely covered with mosaics of porcelain and colored glass. The three ceilings of Thien Dinh Palace are decorated with frescoes depicting the nine dragons hidden in the clouds, executed by craftsman Phan Van Tach. Legend has it that Khai Dinh had a secret tunnel dug connecting his tomb to the Forbidden City.

Hue's garden houses, called nha vuon, are dwellings built of black jackfruit wood by mandarins and wealthy merchants between the 17th and 19th centuries. Their layout follows a strict code: main house facing south according to feng shui principles, surrounded by a walled garden and a pond. About ten of them are open to visitors in the Kim Long district west of the citadel. Push open the door of An Hien House: a three-bay frame structure with 48 lacquered columns, woodwork carved with lucky bats, and a garden with banana trees and frangipanis. You can have tea there with the family who still lives in it—a conversation far more interesting than any information panel.

activities.text: Dong Ba Market, located north of Trang Tien Bridge on the north bank of the Perfume River, has been Hue's central market since the early 19th century. The ground floor is devoted to spices, fresh herbs, and ingredients for royal cuisine: fine Hue chilies, dried shrimp, moc tom shrimp paste. Come between 4pm and 6pm when the bánh khoai vendors—crispy pancakes filled with shrimp and pork—set up under the awnings outside. Don't leave without trying mạch nya, a malt confection typical of Hue that you'll find almost nowhere else in Vietnam.

After visiting the citadel, treat yourself to a stroll along the quays that line the Perfume River—its name comes from the frangipani flowers that fell into its waters in autumn and perfumed the city. Trang Tien Bridge, rebuilt in 1899 by Gustave Eiffel's engineers on six metal spans, is beautifully illuminated in the evening. Small restaurants along the quays offer bánh bèo—steamed rice cakes topped with dried shrimp and fried onions, one of Hue's most typical street specialties—for just a few thousand dong per plate.

Tour operators offer sunset cruises aboard wooden sampans from the quays at the foot of Trang Tien Bridge. These boats sail upstream toward Thien Mu Pagoda as the sun sets over the citadel, then drift back down enjoying the low light on the banks. Plan for between 45 minutes and 1h30 depending on the boat you choose. Some boats offer live nhã nhạc court music on board—check with your accommodation the day before so you don't miss this option.

Hue's cuisine stands apart in Vietnamese gastronomy: inherited from imperial court cooks, it's renowned for its refinement and pronounced taste for chili. Two dishes not to miss tonight: bún bò Huế, beef and pork shank vermicelli soup in a spicy lemongrass broth—radically different from northern pho—and bánh khoai, crispy pancakes filled with shrimp and bean sprouts dipped in peanut sauce. The Dong Ba district and the small alleys around the market concentrate the best informal spots; avoid the overly touristy restaurants around Trang Tien Bridge.

places.name: Kon Tum

visits.text: Built between 1913 and 1918 by French priest Guise Decrouille, Kon Tum's wooden cathedral is considered the finest church in the Central Highlands. It marries European Romanesque style with Bahnar stilt houses: its 100 wooden pillars are meticulously carved, and its stained-glass windows blend Christian motifs with scenes from local life. Behind the building sits Vinh Son Orphanage, which shelters over 700 children from ethnic minority groups, funded by the community's sisters. Come mid-morning to see light streaming through the colored windows and illuminating the wooden pillars.

The Kon Klor suspension bridge spans 292 meters across the Dak Bla River, connecting downtown to the Bahnar and Jarai villages on the opposite bank. Its yellow-orange color stands out against the intense green of surrounding rice paddies and sugarcane fields. On the far side, Kon Klor's communal house (nhà rông) is regarded as the largest Rong house in the Tây Nguyên—entirely built from wood, bamboo, and thatched grass, with a spectacular roof that soars vertically like an axe blade. The best time to visit is late afternoon: the light gilds the bridge cables and reflects in the river at sunset.

The Dak Bla is one of the few rivers in Vietnam to flow from east to west—counter to the majority of Central Highlands watercourses. It rises in Kon Plong district before traveling over 100 kilometers to Kon Tum, where it joins the Po Ko River to form the Se San River. Following its banks, you'll pass through Bahnar villages like Ro Ngao or Kon Ktu, with their vertical-roofed Rong houses and welcoming inhabitants. Plan to walk at least 3 kilometers along the shore to capture everyday life: basket fishing, cassava gardens, children playing in the water.

Sixty kilometers northeast of Kon Tum, Mang Den sits at 1,200 meters elevation on the Truong Son Range plateau. Its name derives from the M'Nam term T'mang Deeng—'residence of the gods'—and legend tells that its seven lakes and three waterfalls were created by the tears of an avenging deity. The climate is cool year-round, between 16 and 20°C, and the vast pine forests—140,000 hectares—are worth the trip alone. The French came here in the early decades of the 20th century intending to build a hill station; their remnants, including a statue of the Virgin and Mang Den church, still stand. The road from Kon Tum via Highway 24 is itself a spectacle—mountain passes, mist-filled valleys, and the first pines appearing from 900 meters up.

Pa Sy waterfall—whose name means 'three streams converging' in the Ro Mam language—is formed by the confluence of Mang Den's two largest watercourses. It cascades in a white veil from a rocky escarpment at around 1,500 meters elevation, deep in a virgin pine forest. A hundred steps lead down to the base of the falls; wear shoes with good grip. Nearby, Dak Ke Lake is the largest natural lake on the Mang Den plateau—you can take a boat ride in near-total silence, surrounded by forests and green hills.

Kon Bring is one of four tourist villages in Kon Plong district, inhabited by the M'Nam people. Here you'll find a restored Rong house, basketry crafts, and rice-wine jars that the elders offer visitors during small welcoming ceremonies. The road there runs alongside terraced rice fields that turn red in September-October—if you're passing then, stop for photos. The M'Nam are also known for cultivating ginseng in the surrounding forests, a local medicinal plant that improves daily life in these still very isolated villages.

activities.text: Situated on the banks of the Dak Bla, Kon Tum Museum brings together artifacts, photos, and documents on the history of the Central Highlands peoples: gongs, Bahnar and Jarai costumes, ritual objects, and traces of colonial history. Kon Tum province is home to over 330,000 inhabitants, the majority belonging to the Bahnar, Jarai, Brau, Gie Trieng, Ro Mam, and Xo Dang groups. If you're here in March or April, ask about the Kon Klor festival celebrating the harvest with dances, gongs, and a food fair—a rare moment to see the villages in celebration.

Bún đỏ is a specialty you'll struggle to find elsewhere in Vietnam: white noodles that turn brick-red after simmering in a broth flavored with annatto, shrimp shells, and local spices. The dish is topped with meat from rice-paddy crabs, pork meatballs, and quail eggs. Local eateries serve it from early morning. Try it for breakfast as the locals do—it's the best time of day to grab a table without a queue.

Mang Den's pine forests are crossed by very accessible trails, and the mountain's fresh air is a radical change from the plains of Vietnam. Set out early in the morning: light filters through the trunks in a light mist and the smell of resin fills the air. Local operators offer guides for routes that cross villages and reach the waterfalls—a good way to navigate this dense forest and not miss viewpoints over the valleys. In December-January, wild cherry trees bloom and give the area a surprisingly Japanese feel.

If your guide can get you into a Bahnar village for the evening, don't miss the chance to taste rượu cần—rice wine drunk through a straw from a large communal jar, a welcoming and solidarity ritual among Central Highlands ethnic groups. Some villages near Mang Den also offer evenings with gong music—the Tây Nguyên gong ensembles have been inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list since 2005. Let your accommodation know in advance: this type of experience requires prior arrangement with a local contact.
