visits.text: On the road between Pu Luong and Mai Chau, Thung Khe Pass rises to about 1,000 meters altitude and offers one of the most striking viewpoints in northwest Vietnam. At this spot, the road carved into the white rock overlooks the entire length of Mai Chau valley - a carpet of rice fields framed by karst mountains. Stop for about twenty minutes: Muong vendors offer grilled corn and sticky rice cooked in bamboo (com lam), ideal for breaking up the journey before descending into the valley.

Mai Chau Valley presents itself as a green basin 150 km southwest of Hanoi, surrounded by karst mountains. It's populated mainly by White Thai people. Lac village has housed generations of five large Thai families for over 700 years. Pom Coong village, whose name means 'drum-hill' in Thai, has about 70 households of two-meter-high wooden stilt houses with large windows opening onto the rice fields. Women there still weave intricate patterned brocades that you can buy directly from them - much more pleasant than the souvenir shops in big cities.

activities.text: Before hitting the road to Mai Chau, leave early in the morning for the loop around Ban Bang - a village circuit that crosses terraced rice fields and forest trails. In the morning, mist often clings to the hillsides for an hour or two, and residents begin their days in the fields: a sober, authentic spectacle you won't encounter on tourist routes. A local guide is recommended to avoid getting lost at the junctions - your accommodation can arrange one the evening before.

Mai Chau valley is flat - and that's rare in northwest Vietnam. The villages of Lac, Pom Coong, and Van are connected by dirt paths that run alongside rice fields and cross a few small streams at fords. Bicycles are available at most accommodations for a handful of dollars per day. Set off without GPS: the valley isn't large, and getting lost has no consequences here. The children who follow you shouting 'hello!' are part of the journey.

In the evenings in the valley villages, Thai families organize traditional dance performances in the large common rooms of the stilt houses. This isn't folklore manufactured for tourists: residents wear costumes they display at real festivals, and the dances - notably the Khen dance, performed with a bamboo wind instrument - tell stories of rural life and agricultural cycles. You'll be invited to dance, and the rice alcohol in the communal jar (Ruou Can, drunk through a straw) always ends up loosening inhibitions.

places.name: Moc Chau

visits.text: Perched at 1,050 meters altitude, the Moc Chau plateau extends 80 kilometers long and 25 kilometers wide, and enjoys a much cooler climate than the rest of the country - average temperatures hover around 18-20°C all year. The tea plant hills draw green S-curves or hearts depending on the plantation, much prized by photographers. Late in the day, when mist descends on the valleys and tea pickers return with their baskets, the atmosphere is particularly beautiful. Arrive on weekdays if you want to avoid crowds of Vietnamese tourists who come up on weekends from Hanoi.

What makes Moc Chau visually spectacular is the succession of floral seasons: in January-February, the plum trees of Na Ka valley - 16 kilometers from town - cover the hills with a white blanket that Hmong residents compare to a first snow. In autumn, fields of yellow mustard and wild sunflowers take over. Some producers have developed techniques to advance flowering by a month, extending the season. If you pass outside these windows, the tea hills remain a beautiful alternative year-round. Check with your accommodation about the current blooms for your travel dates.

activities.text: Moc Chau is one of the few regions in Vietnam to practice large-scale dairy farming, an activity developed in the 1970s. Operators offer guided farm visits where you can see the herds, watch milking, and taste local dairy products - yogurts and cheeses made on-site. It's not the most authentic visit of the trip, but it's a real curiosity in a country where fresh milk is traditionally absent from the diet.

If you pass through on a Sunday, the Pa Co ethnic market, about twenty kilometers from Moc Chau town, brings together Hmong, Thai, and Muong people in a very colorful weekly gathering. They sell loose tea, spices, medicinal herbs, and hand-embroidered textiles. Otherwise, the central market in Moc Chau town is lively in the morning on ordinary days - river fish, wild vegetables, corn alcohol in jugs. Arrive between 7am and 9am for the atmosphere.

places.name: Ninh Binh

visits.text: Before taking the boat at Tam Coc, climb Mua Cave. About 480 steps carved into rock lead to the summit of a limestone karst, where the 360-degree view over Tam Coc valley is breathtaking: the Ngo Dong River snaking between sugar loaves and green rice fields. Leave before 8am to avoid heat and crowds, wear shoes with good grip: some steps are slippery in wet weather.

Phu Hau village, 5 km from Tam Coc landing, sits at the end of an hour-and-a-half bike loop between limestone karsts and rice fields. Its car-free alleys and local temple are virtually empty of tourists. Operators rent bikes in Tam Coc and Ninh Binh hotels - the terrain is flat, accessible to all levels.

Inscribed on UNESCO's list in 2014, Vietnam's first mixed natural-cultural site, Trang An covers 2,168 hectares of limestone karsts and flooded valleys. Excavations have revealed traces of human occupation over 30,000 years. The rowing boat circuit crosses 47 caves and 31 enclosed valleys between nearly vertical cliffs, with royal temples from the Dinh and Tran dynasties built into cliff niches. Choose route #3, the longest (3h), for the most impressive caves.

Hoa Lu was Vietnam's first unified capital, founded in 968 by King Dinh Bo Linh after reunifying the country divided into twelve kingdoms. The capital remained here until 1010. Two temples built in the 17th and 18th centuries mark the location of the old palaces. The site is much less crowded than Trang An and Tam Coc - you'll often be alone with the guardians, and this calm is part of the charm.

Bai Dinh Pagoda, inaugurated in 2010 after ten years of construction, extends over more than 1,000 hectares. On the main avenue, 500 blue stone arhat statues 2 to 2.5 meters each accompany visitors to the main temples. Tam The Temple houses three bronze Buddha statues of 50 tons each representing the Buddha of the past, present, and future. Electric tuk-tuks shuttle from the entrance - use them for the climbs.

activities.text: Before leaving Pu Luong, take advantage of dawn for the Ban Bang loop: a trail that follows terraced rice fields, crosses a bamboo bridge over a stream, and offers views of spinning water wheels. The morning air is cool, buffalo emerge from barns - it's the best time to photograph the valley in mist before hitting the road.

Tam Coc means three caves: the limestone tunnels Hang Ca, Hang Hai, and Hang Ba that boats pass through on the Ngo Dong River. What surprises first-time visitors: the rowers use their feet to manipulate the oars, freeing their hands for other tasks. Avoid the hours between 10am and 2pm - the busiest. Arrive at the landing between 7am and 8am for the best light conditions.

Operators rent bikes in Tam Coc - allow an hour and a half for a loop between limestone karsts, rice fields, and small villages. The terrain is flat and the road uncrowded on weekdays. Stop in villages not marked on tourist maps: residents are welcoming and local temples are deserted.

Ninh Binh is known throughout Vietnam for its mountain goat meat. Goats graze on limestone cliffs - their meat is firmer and less fatty than lowland meat. Restaurants around Tam Coc landing serve grilled goat, hot pot, or salad with fresh herbs. Don't look for signs in English: follow groups of Vietnamese families dining together in the evening.

places.name: Phong Nha

visits.text: After a flight from Hanoi to Dong Hoi (1h15) and 45 km westward by road, you'll reach the small village of Son Trach - gateway to Phong Nha-Ke Bang National Park. Drop your bags and start by walking along the Son River, whose waters take on a surprising emerald-green hue depending on the light. The village remains human-scale, with a main street lined with modest guesthouses and family restaurants where grilled prawns share menu space with pork noodle soup. Arrive before 6pm if you want to catch the sunset over the karst peaks framing the valley.

Bong Lai Valley offers the perfect counterpoint to the caves: no stalactites here, just rice paddies, wild boar farms, and families drying peppercorns by the roadside. Rent a bicycle in the village and head west along the Ho Chi Minh Highway - the landscape quickly opens up to grasslands framed by karst formations. You'll pass the Duck Stop, a farm where you can feed ducks while enjoying a cold beer, and Wild Boar Eco Farm, run by a family raising boars caught in the jungle. Budget 2 to 3 hours for the full circuit, longer if you linger at each stop.

Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2003, Phong Nha-Ke Bang National Park spans over 800 km² of jungle and limestone mountains dating back 400 million years - making it one of the oldest karst massifs in Asia. It harbors hundreds of surveyed caves, including the world's largest by volume, Son Doong. Upon arrival, take time to stop at the park's information center to understand the geology of the place before launching into visits: it makes the formations you'll see in the coming days far more meaningful.

Here's Asia's longest dry cave: 31.4 km in total, discovered from 2005 onward by a local resident who heard wind blowing from a crack in the mountain. Speleologists from the British Cave Research Association later mapped the entire system and christened it Thien Duong - Paradise - so stunned were they by the formations. The standard visit covers 1 km of wooden walkway plunging into a limestone cathedral 72 m high and 150 m wide, studded with stalagmites resembling giant candles. In 2011, the Vietnam Book of Records formalized its two titles: longest dry cave and longest wooden bridge in a cave. Arrive at opening, around 7:30am, to beat the tour groups that descend mid-morning. The cave is 22 km from Son Trach by road.

Board a motorized boat at the Son Trach dock: 5 km along the Son River with buffalo on the banks, then the motor cuts and you glide into the semi-darkness of Phong Nha Cave. The cave stretches 7,729 m long, and its underground river extends 13,969 m - the longest in Vietnam. Visitors only penetrate 1,500 m, enough to see columns nicknamed "the Lion," "the Royal Court," "the Buddha." During the American War, the cave served as a hospital and munitions depot and was heavily bombed. Budget about 2 hours total with the round-trip river journey.

Unlike its neighbors, Dark Cave has no installed lighting: headlamp mandatory, you advance in total darkness among dark rocks - basalt and gray-black limestone - through 6 km of galleries. The highlight is the natural mud bath at the back of a chamber: the mud is so dense it supports your weight and doesn't stick to your skin. After exploring, you kayak across the Chay River to reach the opposite bank. Don't come if you're allergic to wet adventure - bring a change of clothes and keep your phone in the provided waterproof bag.

The National Park's Botanic Garden also serves as a rescue center for wild animals captured by poachers - you may spot macaques in rehabilitation. The road leading there, 15 km from Son Trach, crosses bamboo forests that give a sense of what the park's 800 km² represent. Continue to Mooc Spring, a swimming spot in turquoise water gushing directly from a karst fissure at constant temperature. Arrive early to enjoy the spring before tour groups of Vietnamese families who gather there on weekends.

Rarely visited because it's accessed by a staircase from the main cave entrance, Tien Son is a dry cave with formations ranging from milky white to caramel depending on their iron content. It's virtually deserted outside weekends, making it a welcome contrast after the crowds at Paradise Cave. The combined access from the Son River dock only requires a modest additional ticket. Climb the stairs from the Phong Nha Cave jetty late morning, after doing the cave by boat.

Hang En is the third-largest cave in the world by volume - 100 m high, 180 m wide in places - and you camp inside on a white sand beach bathed by a turquoise river. The outbound trek takes about 4 hours through jungle, with some thirty river crossings, passing through the isolated village of Ban Doong where the Bru-Vân Kiều minorities live. Only one operator is authorized to lead expeditions there, with groups limited to 12 people maximum. If you're considering this option, book several months ahead: dates sell out very early. For those who prefer staying at sea level, the day is devoted to the Botanic Garden, Mooc Spring, and the park's marked trails.

activities.text: Son Trach doesn't have Hoi An's culinary sophistication, but that's part of its charm: small family restaurants where you eat what the river and fields provide. Don't miss the bamboo-charcoal grilled pork, a local specialty, or the rice noodle soup with pork broth. Vendors along the main street also offer seafood barbecues in the evening. Plan to eat early: kitchens often close around 9pm.

Providers in Son Trach rent kayaks for a peaceful paddle down the Son River in late afternoon, when the light is most beautiful on the karsts. You'll pass water buffalo bathing on the banks and fishermen checking their nets. It's an excellent way to glimpse the entrance to Phong Nha Cave from the water without paying admission - the next day, you'll enter by boat. Book your kayak in the morning, as spots fill up quickly in high season.

The Dark Cave zipline spans 400 m above the Chay River. You launch from a wooden tower and land directly at the cave entrance, getting soaked if you don't lift your feet when you hit the water. It's the best way to begin the visit: the adrenaline rush from the flight perfectly preps you for the cave exploration that follows. The full package - zipline, return kayak, mud bath, equipment - is offered as a one-stop ticket by local operators on-site.

After the mud bath inside Dark Cave, you exit and take a kayak to cross the Chay River and return to the starting bank. The water is transparent in dry season and slightly cool, which is welcome after the mud passage. Rope courses suspended above the water let you play a bit more before reaching shore. Avoid October through November: rains turn the river muddy and some activities are suspended.

Providers based at the Botanic Garden offer guided circuits in primary jungle on trails following streams and allowing observation of the canopy. Some 10 km circuits pass karst formations and waterfalls. Local guides - often former poachers converted to ecotourism - possess knowledge of medicinal plants and birds that enriches the walk. Set out early morning: tropical forest is stifling after 10am, and animals show themselves more at dawn.

Before leaving Phong Nha tomorrow for Hue, treat yourself to a proper meal at one of the restaurants on the village's main street. The local specialty is banh xeo - crispy rice crepe with pork and bean sprouts - accompanied by nem lui, grilled beef rolls on lemongrass skewers. Some establishments offer cá rô đồng - whole fried rice-field carp. Evenings in Son Trach stay lively until around 10pm, after which the village returns to its riverside calm.

places.name: Phu Quoc

visits.text: More than 50% of the island's area is protected by a national park classified as a UNESCO biosphere reserve in 2010. It covers 31,422 hectares of primary forest and shelters 30 mammal species, over 200 bird species, and 50 reptile species—including about fifteen protected species. Hiking trails depart from the north of the island, notably toward Mount Chua (603 m, the highest point) or waterfalls accessible only in the rainy season. In the dry season (November-April), the forest is passable on foot without risk. Be aware that trails are poorly marked—go with a local guide for hiking.

At the end of the road running along the northwest coast of the island, Ganh Dau is one of Phu Quoc's last fishing villages where daily life still unfolds as it did before the tourist boom. Sun-dried wooden boats, nets stretched on bamboo poles, stilt houses at the water's edge—the contrast with the hotel complexes in the south is total. Ganh Dau beach is renowned for its coral reefs accessible for snorkeling. In clear weather, you'll glimpse the Cambodian coast (Kampot) about thirty kilometers to the northwest.

Dai Beach, on the northwest coast, is one of the island's least crowded despite its 20 km of white sand. It stretches south from Ganh Dau and the water is shallow for several dozen meters—ideal for children and inexperienced swimmers. The northern section remains virtually untouched by construction. Plan to be there late afternoon: Phu Quoc's west coast faces the sunset, and sunsets from this beach are among the island's most beautiful.

Sao Beach, in the south of the island, is regularly designated as Vietnam's most beautiful beach. Its 7 km of fine white sand and crystalline turquoise water, backed by coconut palms and forest-covered hills, fully justify the reputation. The water is calm and shallow—perfect for family swimming. Arrive before 10am to enjoy the calm: group excursions arrive mid-morning. Several beach restaurants offer seafood grilled on site. If you have a mask and snorkel, some coral reefs accessible by swimming are located at the southern end of the beach.

The Dinh Cau night market, in the heart of Duong Dong, is Phu Quoc's evening gathering place. From 6pm, about a hundred stalls set up along the canal: seafood (oysters, lobsters, squid, Ham Ninh crabs) that you choose live from aquariums and are cooked in front of you, but also souvenirs, Phu Quoc pepper, pearls, and handicrafts. Prices are reasonable if you move away from the first aisles. It's also the best place to buy Phu Quoc fish sauce (nước mắm)—one of the world's most renowned, made for 200 years using traditional methods.

Ong Lang is the west coast's quietest beach—less crowded than Long Beach in the south and more intimate than Bai Dai in the north. A series of small coves separated by rocks makes it particularly pleasant in low season. Bungalows and small boutique hotels line the beach without denaturing it. In the morning, the water is calm and the beach nearly deserted on weekdays. It's the ideal spot for a final swim in the Gulf of Thailand before the afternoon departure.

Phu Quoc is nicknamed 'pearl island'—the Gulf of Thailand waters have been suitable for farming Pinctada maxima pearl oysters for decades. Pearl farms on the east coast (Ham Ninh sector) offer guided tours explaining the complete cycle of pearl culture: from nacre grafting to harvest, including sorting and pearl treatment. The process takes 18 months to 3 years depending on the desired size. At the end of the visit, you can buy directly on site—Phu Quoc cultured pearls are less known than those from Japan but offer good value.

Phu Quoc fish sauce is the only one in Southeast Asia to benefit from protected geographical indication recognized by the European Union. Artisan factories in the Duong Dong quarter use mahogany barrels in which anchovy fish (cá cơm) and sea salt ferment for 12 to 15 months. At the end of fermentation, the sauce is pressed and filtered by hand. The smell in the factories is powerful—some visitors are surprised—but the visit is instructive. Local operators allow you to visit and buy directly on site.

activities.text: Returning to Duong Dong in the evening, don't miss the Dinh Cau rock, a natural formation at the edge of the fishing port on which a temple dedicated to Thien Hau, goddess of the sea venerated by fishermen for her protection, was built in 1937. Twenty-nine carved stone steps lead to the summit, from which you embrace the town of Duong Dong, the port, and the sun plunging into the Gulf of Thailand. It's one of the island's most photogenic spots. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset to secure a good spot.

The best way to explore northern Phu Quoc is by scooter or motorbike, which operators rent by the day from Duong Dong. The main road goes up to Ganh Dau along the west coast with sea views at every turn. A few dirt tracks provide access to isolated beaches. Drive carefully on unpaved sections after rain—laterite can be slippery.

The An Thoi archipelago, at the southern tip of Phu Quoc, groups 15 islets whose seabeds are among Vietnam's richest. The most renowned dive sites are around May Rut, Mong Tay, and Dam Ngang islets—colorful coral reefs, rays, turtles, schools of tropical fish. Local operators offer full-day boat excursions combining 2 or 3 diving and snorkeling stops with lunch on board. Boats depart from An Thoi port, 15 km south of Duong Dong. For scuba diving, book in advance and verify required certifications.

After a morning of diving, return to Bai Sao for the afternoon. Late in the day, when excursions leave around 3-4pm, the beach regains its calm. The water stays warm all day (26-29°C in dry season) and the late afternoon light transforms the coconut palms into golden silhouettes. A few beach bars serve fresh coconuts and fruit juices—settle in until sunset before heading back to Duong Dong for the night market.

Make the most of the final hours before departure with a seafood lunch at one of the beachfront restaurants around Ong Lang or Duong Dong. The specialty to order is steamed Ham Ninh crab with lemongrass—Ham Ninh, a fishing village on the east coast, has been renowned for its crabs for decades. Beachfront restaurants display their daily catch in tanks—you choose your fish live before it's prepared. Plan to be at the airport about 2 hours before your flight time.

Phu Quoc International Airport (PQC) is located in the center of the island, about 10-15 minutes by taxi from most west coast hotels. It's served by several Vietnamese carriers to Ho Chi Minh City, Hanoi, and in recent years by direct international flights. In high season, return flights can be very crowded—check in online if possible to avoid long lines.

visits.text: Six kilometers north of the city center, T'Nung Lake—called Bien Ho by the Vietnamese—fills the crater of a volcano that went dormant millions of years ago. Its deep blue color earned it the nickname 'The Eye of Pleiku'; Jarai legend tells how an entire village was swallowed by the earth on a day of disobedience, and the survivors' tears formed the lake. Local operators offer boat rides across its 250 hectares in a remarkably peaceful setting. Arrive early in the morning, before 8am, to enjoy the still water before Vietnamese tour groups descend around midday.

Just a stone's throw from downtown, Plei Op village is one of the few Jarai communities still accessible on foot from Pleiku. Here you'll see the pointed-roof communal houses (nhà rông) and Jarai collective tombs—these little wooden 'houses of the dead' where the community deposits offerings and statues for several months until the spirit of the deceased finally departs. Jarai society is matrilineal: women inherit property and choose their husbands. Wear respectful clothing, and if possible, come with a French-speaking guide who knows the families—the welcome will be much warmer.

Just north of town, the red volcanic soil gives way to vast tea plantations that stretch almost horizontally for hundreds of meters—an unusual landscape in a region typically more rolling. Buu Minh Pagoda nestles at the heart of these tea hills; built in a very peaceful setting, it offers a pleasant contrast to the bustle of Pleiku market. Take the opportunity to buy tea directly from local growers. Note: according to early-2025 traveler reports, some plots have been uprooted in certain areas to make way for more profitable coffee plants—check the current state of the plantations on-site.

activities.text: Mount Ham Rong—'Dragon's Jaw'—rises 11 kilometers south of Pleiku and reaches over 1,000 meters elevation. From the summit, panoramic views sweep across the city, coffee and rubber-planted hills, and on clear days, the distant ridges of the Truong Son Range. The ascent takes about 45 minutes and presents no major technical difficulty, though trails can be slippery after rain—closed shoes essential. Climb late afternoon for the best light, and check the weather forecast: early morning mist can also make the panorama quite striking.

From 5pm onward, Pleiku's night market comes alive and plunges you into the no-frills Vietnamese life of the highlands. Here you'll find coffee and tea purchased directly from growers in the surrounding area, plus dozens of local specialties to sample on-site. It's also a chance to observe the mix of Jarai, Bahnar, and Kinh ethnic minorities mingling in the aisles. Come hungry: dishes from the red earth lands—grilled meats, rice soups, and marinated meat skewers—are eaten standing up with your feet in the dust, and it's all the better for it.

places.name: Pu Luong

visits.text: The Pu Luong Nature Reserve - 'the highest mountain' in Thai language - extends over more than 17,600 hectares straddling the districts of Ba Thuoc and Quan Hoa, in Thanh Hoa province. It's inhabited primarily by Thai and Muong people who cultivate rice on terraces using methods handed down through generations. What distinguishes Pu Luong from the better-known destinations of northwest Vietnam is its authenticity: few tourists, villages still focused on agriculture, and bamboo water wheels - norias - that still irrigate the rice fields as they have for centuries. The valley is particularly beautiful during the flooded rice paddy season (February-March) and at harvest time (September-October).

Ban Don is considered the most accessible and dynamic village in the reserve: 76 households, wooden stilt houses topped with thatched or palm roofs, and women who still weave brocade by hand according to patterns specific to each family. Ban Hieu, perched higher up, offers a view of the terraced rice fields and a waterfall tumbling into the forest. These villages aren't frozen for tourists: residents live and work there, and that's precisely what makes them interesting. Take the rice field paths rather than the road - the spectacle changes dramatically.

activities.text: Local operators offer bamboo raft descents on the Chàm River, which crosses the reserve's central valley. It's not sporty rafting - the current is calm and navigation is done by pole - but it's a radically different way to see the rice fields and limestone cliffs that border the river. The experience lasts between one and two hours depending on the chosen departure point. Book through your accommodation the day before, as there may be a wait if several groups are present on the same day.

Sleeping in a Thai stilt house in Pu Luong isn't just about comfort - it's access to a world you wouldn't see otherwise. Dinner is shared with the host family: sticky rice cooked in bamboo (com lam), vegetables from the garden, river fish, and often rice alcohol drawn from a communal jar with a bamboo straw. Mosquito nets delineate sleeping spaces in a large common room. There's little or no wifi, electricity can be intermittent, and the rooster crows well before dawn. Those are the rules of the game, and that's why you're here.

places.name: Quy Nhon

visits.text: Halfway between Da Nang and Quy Nhon, Sa Huynh is a coastal village known for its salt pans and especially for archaeological excavations that revealed a prehistoric culture - the "Sa Huynh culture" dating from 1,000 to 200 BC - considered the direct ancestor of Cham civilization. The village also offers a beautiful black volcanic sand beach little frequented by foreign tourists. A brief stop for lunch and leg-stretching is warranted on this long drive day.

Just 3 km from downtown Quy Nhon, Thap Doi towers are the most accessible Cham vestiges in Binh Dinh province. Built between the late 12th and early 13th centuries, these two red brick towers - with Khmer decorative influence visible in the bas-reliefs of garudas and Hindu deities - stand in a small public garden surrounded by palms and banana trees. They were part of a three-tower complex, but the third was never completed for unknown reasons. Binh Dinh still has 14 preserved Cham towers - remnants of the era when Vijaya, the ancient capital of the Champa kingdom, was just kilometers away. Arrive early morning: the site becomes a popular selfie spot with Vietnamese high schoolers by 9am.

Fifteen kilometers northwest of Quy Nhon, the Banh It complex groups four restored Cham towers perched on a hill overlooking the plain and Binh Dinh River. Dating from the late 11th century - a period of architectural transition from My Son A1 style to Binh Dinh style - they're more imposing and less known than Thap Doi. Despite their accessibility from National Route 1A, you'll often have the site to yourself outside weekends. If you have time, the 10-minute hill climb offers a panorama over rice paddies descending to the sea.

Twenty-five kilometers northeast of Quy Nhon, Ky Co Beach is a white sand cove wedged between cliffs on three sides and the sea on the fourth. The water changes color with depth - translucent turquoise near shore, deep blue beyond - and coral reef surfaces in places. To reach it from the fishing village of Nhon Ly, operators offer canoes that cross the bay in 10 to 15 minutes: the crossing itself, between limestone cliffs dropping into the water, is as beautiful as the beach. Arrive before 9am: the light is better and the beach less crowded. Don't stay past 2pm in summer - waves pick up and the last canoes depart.

Eo Gio - "the wind strait" - takes its name from its geography: seen from above, the site resembles a funnel cut between two high cliffs, sucking in winds from the South China Sea in all seasons. Waves crashing on dark rocks for centuries have carved hollows and natural caves into the limestone walls. A coastal path with red and white railings snakes along the cliff and offers plunging views of the sea. Devote about 1h30 to it, preferably early morning when water colors are most beautiful, or late afternoon for sunset.

Between Ky Co and Eo Gio nestles the village of Nhon Ly, whose colorful houses descend to the water's edge. Murals painted on house exteriors make it a photogenic spot, and fishermen mending their nets in the morning readily tolerate the curious. This is where you'll find the boats to Ky Co, and also a few small waterfront restaurants serving grilled clams and lime sea urchins at very reasonable prices. Wander here mid-morning, between two visits.

Thirteen kilometers south of Quy Nhon, Bai Xep is a fishing village with 500 m of coastline encircled by green hills - Business Insider ranked it among "Asia's hidden gems." Unlike Ky Co, no canoe needed: you arrive by motorbike or taxi, park your vehicle, and find yourself on a golden sand beach where fishermen's round boats are pulled ashore by 7am. The water is clear and the cove sheltered enough for safe swimming. One tip: avoid midday in summer - there's virtually no natural shade on the beach, and temperatures often exceed 35°C.

If you didn't have time to see them the day before, the towers of Banh It (15 km northwest) or Canh Tien - "fairy wings" - merit a last stop before the airport. Canh Tien stands within the ancient citadel of Vijaya, capital of the Champa kingdom from 1000 to 1471, dismantled after conquest by King Lê Thánh Tông's Vietnamese army. The tower receives few foreign visitors and the site is remarkably peaceful. From the hill, views extend over rice paddies and the mountains of Binh Dinh's hinterland.

Phu Cat Airport, 35 km north of Quy Nhon, serves Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City with several daily flights. Plan to leave Quy Nhon at least 1h30 before takeoff time. If you prefer the train, Dieu Tri Station is on the main north-south line 10 km northwest of Quy Nhon - a comfortable option toward Da Nang or beyond. Before departing, take a quick tour of Quy Nhon's central market for some specialties to take home: nem chả lụa (fermented pork sausage) and mè xửng (sesame caramel) are the quintessential local food souvenirs.

activities.text: Though Quy Nhon remains little known to foreign tourists, its culinary reputation is solid among Vietnamese for fresh seafood. Providers along the waterfront set up evening stalls with lobster, sea urchins, grilled scallops, and lemongrass clams - the morning catch is served late afternoon. Look for plastic tables set up on sidewalks after 6pm near the night market: that's where local families dine, not in the restaurants with English menus.

Quy Nhon's central beach stretches for several kilometers along a boulevard that stays lively until nightfall. Unlike Da Nang or Nha Trang, the promenade isn't yet disfigured by rows of hotels and remains frequented mainly by residents. Outdoor sports equipment, sugarcane juice vendors, and groups of seniors doing tai chi at sunset give the place an air of Vietnam not yet formatted for export. Ideal for a first impression of the city after a long drive.

Local operators in Nhon Ly charter motorized canoes for the crossing to Ky Co - expect about 15 min travel time between the cliffs. The price is fixed and includes the return. Go early morning on a weekday to have the beach almost to yourself: Ky Co is very popular with Vietnamese on weekends and school holidays, and the beach can then lack space. In rough seas or from September to November, the crossing may be suspended - inquire the day before at your accommodation.

Returning from Ky Co and Eo Gio, make a stop at the small informal restaurants of Nhon Ly village. Lemongrass scallops and beer clams are prepared to order, and whole grilled squid is served with a peanut and chili sauce that fishermen will bring you with visible pride. The view of boats returning to dock in late afternoon completes the picture.

Bai Xep fishermen return to dock early morning - around 5:30 to 7am in summer - and mend their nets on the beach. It's the best time to observe village life before tourists arrive. A few small establishments open at 7am for breakfast with xôi (sticky rice) and cà phê sữa đá (Vietnamese iced coffee with condensed milk). Don't miss the round bamboo boats - thung chai - used to get from shore to fishing boats anchored further out.

Quy Nhon's central market, in the heart of the city, brims with local souvenirs to take home. Quy Nhon nem - fermented meat rolls wrapped in banana leaves - are the specialty most sought after by Vietnamese travelers. Mè xửng, sesame and cashew caramel packaged in small boxes, travels well by plane. A few craft shops around the market also offer Cham embroidery and ceramics from the region.

places.name: Sapa

visits.text: Perched at 1,600 meters altitude in the Hoang Lien Son range, Sapa was a French hill station founded in 1903. The remaining colonial buildings - stone church from 1930, former governor's residence - coexist today with modern hotels and the stalls of Hmong women who come down each morning to sell brocades and silver jewelry. Walk around the central market when you arrive: that's where you'll see city residents and ethnic villagers side by side, before the tourist crowd takes over around 10am.

Cat Cat village is located just 3 kilometers from central Sapa - a half-hour descent on foot via a trail that crosses rice fields. Formed in the 19th century by Black Hmong families, the village preserves traditional craft practices: women weave and embroider indigo-dyed clothing, and demonstrators still show the dyeing work on-site. A hydroelectric plant built by the French is still operating beside the waterfall. It's the most touristic village in the region - for something more authentic, save your energy for the villages of Muong Hoa valley the next day. But for a quick first immersion when arriving in Sapa, Cat Cat does the job very well.

Muong Hoa Valley extends for about fifteen kilometers southeast of Sapa, between 900 and 1,600 meters altitude. The terraced rice fields covering its slopes are ranked among the most spectacular in Southeast Asia: each level has been carved by hand by successive generations of Hmong farmers, and the flooded terraces of May-June or golden ones in September-October change color and texture with each hour of the day. Interpretive signs in several languages explain the traditional irrigation system. Take time to sit by the edge of a canal before setting off on your trek - the spectacle deserves five minutes of stillness.

Muong Hoa Valley is the backbone of trekking in Sapa. It extends about fifteen kilometers and houses two of the most interesting villages in the region. Lao Chai is a Black Hmong village - the indigo-dyed clothing of the women stains hands if you touch it, and that's not a bad way to start a conversation. Ta Van, two kilometers away, is a Giay village whose wooden stilt houses are built differently from those of neighboring Hmong. Between the two, rice terraces descend in wide steps toward the valley floor. The trail connecting the two villages crosses wooden bridges, cardamom fields, and a few small streams at fords. Count on 4 to 5 hours for the complete loop from Sapa.

activities.text: Sapa sits at 1,600 meters altitude on the slope of the Hoang Lien Son range. When the sky is clear - which isn't guaranteed - the view of Muong Hoa valley from viewpoints above town is striking: 15 kilometers of terraced rice fields stacked on the slopes, dotted with Hmong and Dao hamlets. In late afternoon, the oblique light on the flooded terraces (May-June) or golden stalks (September-October) yields photographs you'll remember for a long time. If fog arrives - and it often does - don't worry: Sapa's misty atmosphere has its own admirers.

In the evening in Sapa, Black Hmong and Red Dao women come down to town and set up on the sidewalks of the central square to sell their embroidery by lamplight. It's not an organized market - it's simply their daily way of earning a bit of money after the day. Take time to sit beside them, even without buying. The atmosphere of this square in the evening, with mists descending from the mountains and the sounds of the night market, is one of the most distinctive images of the entire journey.

Fansipan peaks at 3,143 meters, making it the highest point in Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia combined - hence the nickname 'Roof of Indochina.' The cable car inaugurated in 2016, one of the longest in the world, connects the Sapa station to the summit in 15 minutes. At the top: a giant Buddha statue, several pagodas, and in clear weather, a panorama of the Hoang Lien Son range. Dress warmly - it's generally 5 to 10°C cooler than in town, and fog can arrive without warning. If you prefer exertion, the ascent on foot takes two to three days with a mandatory guide.

Bac Ha, 110 kilometers from Sapa, hosts every Sunday one of the most colorful and least artificial ethnic markets in northern Vietnam. The town is the capital of the Flower Hmong - whose women wear embroidered costumes predominantly orange that artisans take 4 to 6 months to make. More than 14 ethnic groups gather there each Sunday, with stalls of meat, medicinal plants, spices, corn wine, silver jewelry, and brocade. Arrive before 10am - that's when tour buses arrive. If your day 7 isn't a Sunday, skip it; the village without the market has little interest.
